title: Delhi Launches ‘Heat Smart Schools’ Amid Rising Heatwave Concerns - APAC Media
full_text:
New Delhi, July 16 (APAC Media): Delhi has launched a ‘Heat Smart School’ pilot project to make schools safer for students during extreme heat and heatwaves, with the first model implemented at Sarvodaya Bal Vidyalaya, BT Block, Shalimar Bagh.
The pilot was reviewed on Wednesday by officials from the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA), the Delhi Government, GIZ India, and other partner organisations.
The initiative is aimed at developing climate-resilient schools through practical and scalable interventions.
Cool Roofs, Shaded Areas and Better Drinking Water
The pilot school has been equipped with several heat-mitigation measures, including:
These interventions are designed to reduce heat stress and provide a safer learning environment during periods of extreme heat.
CM Rekha Gupta Backs Expansion
Chief Minister Rekha Gupta said protecting children’s health and well-being remains a top priority for the Delhi Government. She said the Heat Smart School model addresses the growing impact of climate change and expressed confidence that it would be expanded to more government schools across the Capital.
Part of Delhi’s Heat Action Plan
District Magistrate Shailendra Singh Parihar said the initiative is an extension of the Delhi Heat Action Plan, translating climate policy into practical interventions at the school level.
NDMA Member Secretary Krishna S. Vatsa said heatwaves are among India’s fastest-growing climate risks and stressed the need to integrate climate adaptation into public infrastructure, including schools.
The Heat Smart School pilot is being implemented under the Integrated Climate Risk Management for Resilient Infrastructure and Communities (InCRIS) project, which focuses on strengthening climate resilience through policy support, capacity building, digital tools and pilot initiatives.
